_provenance.note: Conjecture evaluation loop (Epic Q): the analogical conjectures generated by graphstore.transfer (Epic P) judged for plausibility by an IN-SESSION Claude Code sub-agent acting as a skeptical ML-theory expert. No API call (Max-plan in-session sub-agent), per the project methodology that NLP/judgment steps must use in-session sub-agents. Produced once in-session and committed; graphstore.evaluate validates this artifact DETERMINISTICALLY (schema + that every evaluated conjecture's projection is one the transfer pipeline actually generates), so an evaluation can never drift from the system's real output. Each entry is keyed to the exact cross-domain analogy and projected fact it judges. Extended in Epic AF with C5-C7: the conjectures the fifth literature (online learning / regret, Epic AC) projected across the web, judged by the same in-session sub-agent discipline and gated identically.
_provenance.evaluated_by: in-session Claude Code sub-agent (general-purpose): C1-C4 on 2026-06-27, C5-C7 on 2026-07-01
_provenance.judged_pipeline: graphstore.transfer: C1-C4 over the three-literature graph (conformal, optimization, learning), 2026-06-27; C5-C7 over the five-literature multidomain graph (+ concentration, online learning), 2026-07-01

evaluations.statement: The conformal prediction/calibration procedure has a fixed point in the sense of a self-consistent prediction set.
evaluations.reasoning: Full (transductive) conformal prediction is genuinely a self-consistency construction: a candidate label y is included in the set iff augmenting the data with (x,y) and recomputing nonconformity scores leaves y non-extreme, which is exactly a fixed-point/consistency condition on the augmented empirical distribution. The analogy is more an apt re-description than a Banach contraction, however, since conformal's self-consistency is a thresholding condition, not the unique attractor of an iterated kappa-Lipschitz map, and uniqueness/contraction need not hold. So the projected fixed point lands on a real existing structure but the mechanism (contraction with kappa<1) does not transfer.
evaluations.related_known_work: full/transductive conformal prediction self-consistency (Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer); split conformal as the non-self-referential variant

evaluations.statement: An importance/likelihood-ratio reweighting between distributions underlies control of a contraction map's modulus.
evaluations.reasoning: There is a legitimate but narrow correspondence: in Markov-chain and MDP theory the contraction modulus of the transition/Bellman operator is governed by overlap between distributions, and change-of-measure / Radon-Nikodym (likelihood-ratio) bounds appear in concentrability coefficients and Dobrushin/Doeblin coupling conditions that yield geometric contraction. But generic Banach contraction (e.g., gradient descent on a strongly convex smooth function, kappa=1-mu/L) has a modulus set by curvature/conditioning with no reweighting structure at all, so the projection is false as a general claim. It is a real phenomenon in distribution-coupling settings and a non-sequitur elsewhere, hence genuinely interesting only when restricted.
evaluations.related_known_work: Dobrushin/Doeblin coefficients and coupling for Markov-chain geometric ergodicity; concentrability / change-of-measure coefficients in approximate dynamic programming; importance-weighted/covariate-shift conformal (Tibshirani et al. 2019)

evaluations.statement: A finite-capacity / complexity-control premise underlies weighted exchangeability and hence conformal coverage.
evaluations.reasoning: This conflates two unrelated mechanisms: conformal coverage follows from the symmetry/exchangeability of the rank statistic and holds in finite samples for ANY (even infinite-capacity or adversarially complex) score function, with no VC or capacity assumption whatsoever — that distribution-free property is conformal's defining selling point. Weighted exchangeability (Tibshirani et al.) is an algebraic property of the joint law under reweighting, not a uniform-convergence condition, so finite VC dimension is neither necessary nor sufficient for it. The structure-mapping put capacity control in the slot of the property that earns the guarantee, but in conformal the guarantee is earned by exchangeability instead, so the projection is largely spurious.
evaluations.related_known_work: distribution-free finite-sample validity of conformal prediction (Vovk et al.); weighted exchangeability (Tibshirani et al. 2019); VC uniform convergence (Vapnik-Chervonenkis) as the contrasting non-transferred mechanism

evaluations.statement: The ERM / uniform-convergence operator has a fixed point.
evaluations.reasoning: Uniform convergence is a concentration statement (sup over a class of |empirical - true risk|), not an operator with a domain mapped to itself, so fixed point is not even type-correct for it. There is a real but different sense in which the projection lands: many learning algorithms ARE fixed-point iterations — gradient descent/EM/self-consistent estimating equations converge to a stationary point of the empirical risk, and population risk minimizers satisfy first-order fixed-point conditions. So the conjecture is false as literally about the uniform-convergence operator but points at a true adjacent fact about risk-minimization dynamics.
evaluations.related_known_work: fixed-point iteration of estimating equations / EM; stationary points of empirical risk via gradient flow; Banach fixed-point theorem; uniform convergence (Vapnik-Chervonenkis) as the mismatched object

evaluations.statement: The no-regret online play has a fixed point - a limit object the play converges to.
evaluations.reasoning: The Banach projection invents a unique attracting fixed point for the iterates of no-regret play, and as a general claim this is false: no-regret guarantees control time-averaged loss, not iterate convergence, and last-iterate behavior of OGD/FTRL in games generically cycles or diverges (zero-sum bilinear games being the canonical counterexample). What actually converges is a different object: the empirical distribution of play converges to the set of coarse correlated equilibria (a set, not a Banach fixed point), and in single-objective OCO the averaged iterate converges to the minimizer via online-to-batch. However, in restricted regimes the projection is literally correct: for strongly monotone games or a fixed strongly convex loss, the OGD update operator is a genuine contraction and its Banach fixed point (the equilibrium/minimizer) attracts the last iterate. So the conjecture is a real phenomenon under curvature/monotonicity assumptions but conflates averaged-play limit sets with contraction fixed points in general.
evaluations.related_known_work: Folk theorem on no-regret play converging to coarse correlated equilibria (Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi; Hart & Mas-Colell); last-iterate non-convergence/cycling of no-regret dynamics (Bailey & Piliouras, Mertikopoulos et al.); last-iterate convergence in strongly monotone games and contraction view of GD.

evaluations.statement: The weighted conformal calibration procedure is a no-regret play: bounded loss gradients over the reweighted calibration data drive no-regret behavior of the calibration/test procedure.
evaluations.reasoning: This projection lands almost exactly on adaptive conformal inference: Gibbs and Candes recast conformal calibration under distribution shift as an online (sub)gradient descent on the pinball/quantile loss, where the gradient is bounded by max(alpha, 1-alpha), and it is precisely this bounded-gradient structure that yields regret bounds and long-run coverage guarantees without exchangeability. Subsequent work makes the no-regret framing explicit, treating online conformal as an OCO problem with regret and dynamic-regret guarantees. The engine's causal skeleton CAUSE(bounded gradients, no regret) is the correct OGD mechanism transplanted to the correct neighboring literature. The main defect is in the bindings: the target paper (Lei & Candes 2020) is a batch procedure, and the likelihood-ratio weights are fixed density ratios, not learned iterates achieving regret - the true no-regret object is the adaptively updated quantile/coverage level, not the_weights. So the specific instantiation is type-loose, but the claim it points at is established mathematics.
evaluations.related_known_work: Adaptive conformal inference (Gibbs & Candes 2021, 2022); conformal prediction as online learning with regret guarantees (Bhatnagar et al. strongly adaptive online conformal; Angelopoulos, Candes & Tibshirani conformal PID control; Zaffran et al. ACI with OGD); weighted conformal under covariate shift (Tibshirani et al. 2019; Lei & Candes 2020).

evaluations.statement: A curvature (strong-convexity) bound causes the offline gradient-descent map to behave as a no-regret play with modulus kappa.
evaluations.reasoning: The statement is type-incorrect as written: 'no-regret' is a property of a sequential player facing a stream of adversarial losses, not of a fixed offline gradient-descent map, so NO_REGRET(the_map, kappa) does not parse as a theorem. But the causal skeleton points at a true and well-known correspondence: strong convexity (a curvature bound with modulus kappa) simultaneously makes online gradient descent achieve O(log T) regret and makes the offline GD map a kappa-dependent contraction with linear convergence - the same curvature quantity drives both, and online-to-batch conversion turns the log-regret bound into an O(log T / T) batch convergence rate. Read charitably, the conjecture is the regret-to-convergence bridge with the wrong type on the conclusion; read literally, it attributes a regret property to a deterministic operator. The modulus kappa is genuinely the shared parameter, which is why this projection half-lands.
evaluations.related_known_work: Logarithmic regret for strongly convex online learning (Hazan, Agarwal & Kale 2007); online-to-batch conversion (Cesa-Bianchi, Conconi & Gentile; Cutkosky's anytime online-to-batch); linear convergence/contraction of GD under strong convexity (Polyak, Nesterov).
